summary: This post demonstrates how to use TLA+ to model and verify a Go concurrency bug originally described by Chris Siebenmann. The author walks through translating Go code with goroutines, channels, and defer statements into a PlusCal specification, showing how to model both buffered and unbuffered channels. The TLA+ model checker successfully reproduces the deadlock and verifies that three proposed fixes all work correctly. The post concludes by noting that while TLA+ works for this purpose, Spin might be better suited for Go channel semantics since it has channels as a native datatype.
key_points: Go's unbuffered channels require special handling in TLA+ because they need both sender and receiver to be ready simultaneously, which doesn't map cleanly to PlusCal's single-process-per-step model
Goroutine spawning can be emulated in TLA+ by pre-defining all processes with an 'initialized' flag that they await before executing
The deadlock occurs because goroutines can only return tokens after writing to the unbuffered 'found' channel, but main only reads from 'found' after initializing all goroutines
About 75 lines of TLA+ specification verified 20 lines of Go code, with over half being reusable channel logic
Spin may be more appropriate than TLA+ for modeling Go channels since it has channels as a native datatype and was previously used to model Go's runtime scheduler
money_quotes: But just knowing the syntax isn't enough to write specs, and it helps to have examples to draw from.

For the purposes of pedagogy I'm not modeling what we actually read or write. This is good practice when writing real-world specs too: write the simplest specification that usefully captures behavior and iteratively add detail to that.
The goroutines can't return tokens until all goroutines are initialized, and main can't initialize all goroutines until some goroutines have returned their tokens.
Noneless, writing the TLA+ version wouldn't be significantly more effort than writing the original version and could save you net time if it caught the deadlock before production.
Obviously I'm inclined towards using TLA+, given it's my professional language of choice. However, I suspect that TLA+ isn't exactly the right tool for modeling Go channels.
key_insight: Formal specification with TLA+ can catch subtle concurrency bugs like deadlocks before they reach production, and the effort to write specs is comparable to writing the original code while being reusable across similar problems.
